phase1.output: (Window with beautifully styled controls using inline CSS)
phase1.explanation:
CSS styling makes your UI look professional:

Inline CSS:
• node.setStyle("-fx-property: value;")
• Quick and easy for simple styling

JavaFX CSS Properties:
• -fx-background-color — background color
• -fx-text-fill — text color
• -fx-font-size — font size
• -fx-font-weight — bold, normal
• -fx-padding — padding around content
• -fx-border-color — border color
• -fx-border-width — border thickness
• -fx-border-radius — rounded corners
• -fx-background-radius — background corner radius
• -fx-effect — drop shadow, glow

Pseudo-classes (interactive styles):
• :hover — mouse over
• :focused — has focus
• :disabled — disabled state
• :selected — selected state

External Stylesheets:
• scene.getStylesheets().add("styles.css")
• Better for larger applications
• Reusable styles

CSS File Example (styles.css):
.button {
    -fx-background-color: #3498db;
    -fx-text-fill: white;
    -fx-font-size: 14px;
}
.button:hover {
    -fx-background-color: #2980b9;
}

Common design patterns:
• Consistent colors
• Hover effects for feedback
• Rounded corners for modern look
• Drop shadows for depth

phase2.errorMessage: (Button appears with default color — CSS is ignored or not applied!)
phase2.explanation:
JavaFX uses -fx- properties, not standard CSS properties!

Wrong: "color: red;"
Correct: "-fx-text-fill: red;"

Common mistakes:
• color → -fx-text-fill
• background-color → -fx-background-color
• border → -fx-border-color, -fx-border-width
• padding → -fx-padding
• font-size → -fx-font-size

Always use the -fx- prefix for JavaFX CSS properties!

phase3.prompt:
Write a JavaFX program with:
1. A Label with style: font-size 18px, bold, blue text, padding 10px
2. A Button with style: green background, white text, rounded corners, padding 10px 20px
3. A TextField with style: light gray background, border, padding 8px
4. A VBox with style: background color #f5f5f5, padding 20px

Use inline CSS (setStyle()) for all styling.
